questions.question: What is the most important first question when choosing a cache pattern?
options: Which cache brand the team already knows best.
Which store is authoritative and how reads or writes interact with it.
Whether the API is public or private.
How many dashboards the team wants.
questions.explanation: The source of truth and write path determine whether a cache pattern is safe, not just whether it is fast.

questions.question: Why is a read-heavy workload often a good fit for read-through caching?
options: Because misses can populate the cache while the system of record remains authoritative.
Because it guarantees zero miss latency.
Because it removes the need for invalidation entirely.
Because it makes writes asynchronous by default.
questions.explanation: Read-through fits when misses are acceptable and caching mainly protects the hot read path.

questions.question: When is write-through most justified?
options: When read-after-write freshness matters enough to pay extra work on the write path.
When writes are rare and correctness never matters.
When the cache is cheaper than the database.
When invalidation events are impossible to build.
questions.explanation: Write-through is often chosen to tighten freshness after mutation, but it raises write-path complexity and latency.

questions.question: What is the main danger of write-behind?
options: It usually increases read latency above origin-only serving.
It can hide delayed durability or dropped async updates if the deferred path is not reliable.
It prevents batching completely.
It only works with browser caches.
questions.explanation: Write-behind can be efficient, but only if the delayed persistence path is durable and observable.

questions.question: Which trade-off is most accurate for read-through?
options: Simple cache ownership and good miss amortization, but hot misses can still hammer the origin.
Perfect freshness and no miss path risk.
No need for source-of-truth versioning.
Guaranteed lower total cost regardless of workload.
questions.explanation: Read-through keeps the source authoritative, but miss behavior still matters a lot for hot keys.

questions.question: What metric would best reveal trouble in a write-behind system?
options: Queue lag or age of oldest pending write.
Only average response body size.
The number of markdown files in the repo.
Edge POP count.
questions.explanation: If deferred writes back up, the system risks delayed persistence and possibly visible inconsistency.

questions.question: Which answer sounds strongest in an interview?
options: I will add a cache in front of the database and decide later how writes work.
I will use read-through for the hot read path, then selectively use write-through only where read-after-write freshness matters.
I will use write-behind for every path because async is always more scalable.
I will skip naming the source of truth to stay flexible.
questions.explanation: That answer ties pattern choice to requirements instead of treating caches as interchangeable.

questions.question: Which failure mode most clearly points to missing invalidation or version discipline?
options: Repeated stale reads after the source of truth has already accepted an update.
A temporary drop in static asset compression ratio.
A one-time burst of client retries on login.
Fewer dashboards than expected.
questions.explanation: Persistent stale reads after confirmed writes usually mean the cache is not being refreshed or invalidated correctly.
